title: JavaInsight
summaryLine: A local JavaFX desktop tool that scans Java projects for similar methods, sensitive keywords, and unused variables, then turns the findings into an explainable quality report.

sections.title: Why I Built It
sections.text: I built JavaInsight after noticing a problem that often appears in student team projects. When several beginner developers work on the same Java project, similar helper methods can be written in different classes before the team has discussed a shared structure. The project may still run, but the code becomes harder to maintain, debug, and refactor later. JavaInsight was my way of exploring how a small desktop tool could make this kind of hidden redundancy easier to spot. Instead of only comparing method names, it looks at method behaviour and shows possible similarities in a way that is easier to review. I also added sensitive keyword and unused variable checks as practical reminders for students and junior developers to keep basic code hygiene in mind.

sections.title: Workflow
list: Scan the folder and its subfolders for Java files
Run the three static analysis checks
Review similar methods, sensitive keywords, and unused variables
View the score, grade, and charts
Preview or export the PDF report

sections.title: Key Features
list: Folder scanning for Java source files
Similar method detection based on AST-derived behavioural signals
Sensitive keyword checks in variable names and string literals
Custom keyword list for project-specific checks
Unused variable detection for fields, parameters, and local variables
Score and grade summary based on issue density and penalties
JavaFX result panels with charts and grouped findings

sections.title: Architecture Design
sections.text: For the system structure, JavaInsight is organised around three main parts. The JavaFX interface is responsible for user interaction, including folder selection, scan actions, result panels, score charts, and report preview. Behind the interface, the analysis modules handle the actual code inspection. They scan Java files, parse code structure, and run separate checks for similar methods, sensitive keywords, and unused variables. The findings are then passed into the report side of the tool, where they are grouped into a quality report and used by both the on-screen result panels and the PDF export.

title: Job Application Tracker
summaryLine: A local Spring Boot web app for organising job applications, statuses, notes, and role-specific attachments in one place.

details.whyBuilt: I built this project from a real problem in my own job search. As applications, statuses, notes, CVs, and cover letters started to spread across different places, I wanted one simple system to keep the full application workflow organised.
keyFeatures: Dashboard summary by region and application status
Application records with search and filtering
Region and country grouping for different job markets
Per-user data isolation for applications and documents
Optional company auto-fill using Wikipedia and Wikidata
Local Docker setup for PostgreSQL and MinIO
details.architecture: For the application structure, I kept the project split into three main layers. Controllers handle actions from the Thymeleaf pages, services keep the main application rules, and mappers connect the app with the database. I used this structure because it was close to the Java backend style I had seen during internship work. Keeping the controller, service, and mapper responsibilities separate made the project easier for me to organise, test, and maintain as it grew.
details.database: For this project, I used two types of storage. PostgreSQL stores the structured data, such as users, job application records, statuses, regions, and notes. MinIO is used to store uploaded files, such as CVs and cover letters. For the database tables, I separated the data into three main parts. APP_USERS stores the account information, APPLICATIONS stores the job application records, and DOCUMENTS stores the uploaded document records. I designed it this way because these three parts match the real way I expected the tool to be used, where one user can have many applications, and each application can have related documents. This lets the app connect each uploaded file back to the right user and job application without mixing everything into one table.
